Bagged in the first part of this scenario the author is dealt two cards and must calculate the probability of getting at least one card of a specific suit in the next five cards dealt. The deck has 52 cards and two are already held. The remaining 50 cards are in play. The author needs to find the probability of drawing at least one heart in the next five cards. This can be calculated using combinatorial functions. The total number of ways to choose 5 cards from the remaining 50 is represented by the combination C(50,5). The number of ways to choose 5 cards without any hearts is C(39,5) because there are 39 non-heart cards left. The probability is then 1 minus the probability of drawing no hearts. The closed form expression is 1 - C(39,5)/C(50,5). This formula gives the exact probability of getting at least one heart in the next five cards.

Bagged in the second part of this scenario the author evaluates two strategies to determine which one has the highest expected value. Strategy A offers a 0.4 chance to win $1000 and a 0.6 chance to lose $500. Strategy B offers a 0.6 chance to win $800 and a 0.4 chance to lose $200. The expected value for Strategy A is calculated as 0.4*1000 + 0.6*(-500) which equals 400 - 300 = 100. The expected value for Strategy B is 0.6*800 + 0.4*(-200) which equals 480 - 80 = 400. Based on the highest expected value the author should choose Strategy B. The palindrome in this response is bag.
